content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

August 23, 2016

Mary Neilan
19070 Robinson Rd
Sonoma, CA 95476

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-16-153

Dear Ms. Neilan:

This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹

Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of interest or Section 1090. Moreover, this letter is based on the facts presented. The Fair Political Practices Commission does not act as a finder of fact when it renders advice. (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.)

QUESTION

May you seek appointment to the General Manager position of the Public Agency Risk Sharing Authority of California ("PARSAC")?

CONCLUSION

While the conflict of interest provisions of the Act do not prohibit you from applying for PARSAC's General Manager position, you may not make, participate in making, or influence in your official capacity any governmental decisions concerning the recruitment or hiring of the General Manager.

FACTS

You are City Manager for the City of Belvedere. In July 2015, the City of Belvedere joined PARSAC, a joint powers authority that provides general liability, employment liability and workers compensation coverage for member agencies. Each of its 37 members (cities, towns and one fire

¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-16-153
Page No. 2

district) must appoint a representative to the Board of Directors. You are Belvedere's
representative. Belvedere's Administrative Services Director serves as Alternate Director.

The Board of Directors elects 11 of the 37 members to serve on the Executive Committee.
You have stated that you are not a member of the Executive Committee, and that you did not vote
to appoint any of the current members.

In your request, you indicated that the current PARSAC General Manager has served for 12
years, and has announced plans to retire. The Executive Committee, with the help of a Succession
Management Committee, has been tasked with conducting the recruitment of a new General
Manager. You have also indicated that you have not participated in any of the meetings or
discussions concerning this recruitment. The full Board is not scheduled to meet until December
2016, so it is unclear what role, if any, they will play in the selection. In a telephone conversation,
you indicated that the General Manager position is a full time, salaried position. You would like to
apply for the position of General Manager with PARSAC, and, if offered the position, you would
leave your current position as City Manager to accept the position with PARSAC.

ANALYSIS

Section 87100 of the Act prohibits a public official from making, participating in making, or
otherwise using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which the
official has a financial interest. Section 82048 defines "public official" as every member, officer,
employee, or consultant of a state or local government agency.

A public official has a "financial interest" in a governmental decision if it is reasonably
foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect on one or more of the official's
interests. (Section 87103.) Section 87103 of the Act lists several types of financial interests that can
give rise to a conflict of interest; the two most relevant to your situation are:

• Any source of income, including promised income, from which the official has received
income of $500 or more within 12 months before the decision. (Section 87103(c).)

• An interest in the official's personal finances, including those of the official's immediate
family. This is known as the "personal financial effects" rule. (Section 87103; Regulation
18702.5.)

As the City Manager receiving a salary from the City, you have an interest in the City as a
source of income. However, the Act's definition of income expressly excludes salary and
reimbursement for expenses and per diem received from a state, local, or federal government
agency. (Section 82030(b)(2)).

A public official always has a financial interest in his or her personal finance or the personal
finances of his or her immediate family. For a financial effect on an official's personal finances,
Regulation 18702.5 provides that the financial effect of a decision is material if the official or the
official's immediate family member will receive a measurable financial benefit or loss from the
decision. Thus, an effect on an official's governmental salary may still be disqualifying if there is a
material and foreseeable financial effect on the official's personal finances. However, so long as

File No. A-16-153
Page No. 3

you do not make, participate in making, or influence any governmental decisions (in your official capacity) concerning the recruitment or hiring of the General Manager, you will not violate the Act's conflict of interest provisions by simply applying for, or being appointed to, the position.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Hyla P. Wagner
General Counsel

[signature]

By: Zachary W. Norton
Senior Counsel, Legal Division

ZWN:jgl
